mnemonics.title: Array Index Starts at 0
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER: A in ARRAY stands for ALWAYS START AT ZERO - like counting from ZERO, not ONE!

mnemonics.title: Array Declaration Order
mnemonics.content: DATA TYPE FIRST, THEN NAME WITH SIZE: Think 'TYPE NAME[SIZE]' - like 'int numbers[10]' - not the other way!

mnemonics.title: Row-Major Order
mnemonics.content: ROWS BEFORE COLUMNS: Think R-C (Row-Column) = Right Choice for memory!

mnemonics.title: Array Size Calculation
mnemonics.content: TOTAL = DIM1 × DIM2 × DIM3... - Always MULTIPLY all dimensions!

acronyms.expansion: Aggregated Related Resources Arranged Your Way Systematically arrays
acronyms.usage: Remember store data of SAME type in contiguous memory

acronyms.expansion: Length Of Organized Processing for Arrays
acronyms.usage: Use loops to iterate through array elements

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a single row of lockers numbered 0, 1, 2, 3... Each locker holds one item of the same type.
Picture a classroom seating arrangement - rows and columns. Row 0, Column 0 is the front-left seat.
Think of a book with multiple pages, where each page is a 2D grid. Or a building with floors (3rd dimension), each floor has rooms (2nd dimension), each room has shelves (1st dimension).
Think of hotel rooms in a single floor - room numbers are consecutive (101, 102, 103...), not scattered randomly.

analogies.analogy: Like a pill organizer with fixed compartments - each compartment is the same size, holds same type (pills), and has a numbered position.
Like house numbers on a street - houses are at consecutive addresses (1, 2, 3...), and you can go directly to house number 5 without checking houses 1-4.
Like a chess board - 8×8 grid where each square can hold a piece. You identify a square by its row and column (e.g., E4).
